title: Quiz: Circuit Equivalence Checking
description: Test your understanding of circuit equivalence verification methods.

questions.question: Why is global phase important in circuit equivalence?

options.text: Circuits can differ by e^(i*phi)*I and still be physically equivalent
It changes the number of qubits

questions.explanation: Global phase is unobservable. Two unitaries U1 and U2 are equivalent if U1 = e^(i*phi) * U2.

questions.question: To check if U1 and U2 are equivalent up to global phase, we compute:

options.text: U1^dag @ U2 and check if proportional to identity

questions.explanation: If U1^dag @ U2 = e^(i*phi) * I, then U1 and U2 differ only by global phase.

questions.question: What is the main limitation of direct unitary comparison?

options.text: It requires exponential memory in number of qubits
It only works for single-qubit gates

questions.explanation: Unitary matrices have dimension 2^n x 2^n, requiring exponential memory and computation.

questions.question: HZH = X is a valid circuit identity.
questions.explanation: Applying H, then Z, then H produces the same result as applying X.

questions.question: Simulation-based equivalence checking works by:

options.text: Comparing outputs on random input states

questions.explanation: By testing on many random inputs, we can probabilistically verify equivalence without storing full unitaries.

questions.question: Decision diagram methods for equivalence checking are advantageous because:

options.text: They are always faster than simulation
They exploit circuit structure for memory efficiency
They can only check single-qubit circuits

questions.explanation: Decision diagrams compactly represent structured unitaries, avoiding full matrix storage.

questions.question: When verifying an optimization, what indicates success?

options.text: The optimized circuit has more gates
The circuits are equivalent and gate count is reduced

questions.explanation: A successful optimization preserves circuit behavior (equivalence) while reducing resources (fewer gates).
